title: Between the Petals
synopsis: When young selkie Selkie overhears a conversation not meant for her ears in a blooming cherry orchard, she discovers that the annual swimming competition—and perhaps everything she believes—might not be what it seems. Through repeated visits to the same enchanted grove, she must navigate the treacherous waters between truth and rumor.

chapters.title: The Wrong Branch
chapters.content:
The cherry blossoms fell like whispered secrets, each petal carrying something I probably shouldn't know.

I pressed my soft grey-brown sealskin cloak tighter around my shoulders and tried to make myself smaller against the ancient bark. The voices drifted through the pink-heavy air, mixing with the sweet scent that made everything feel dreamlike and distant.

"—not supposed to tell anyone yet," someone was saying. A voice I thought I recognized but couldn't quite place. "The competition this year—"

"Shh." Another voice, sharper. "Someone might hear."

I held my breath, which was always easier for me than it should have been. My deep sea-dark eyes stayed fixed on the blossoms above, pretending I was just a young selkie enjoying the spring bloom. Which I was, sort of. Except for the part where I was accidentally eavesdropping.

"But the swimmers don't know," the first voice continued. "They're all training so hard, and meanwhile we're—"

A branch cracked somewhere to my left. Both voices went silent.

I waited, counting the petals that landed on my long black hair. One, two, three. When I finally heard footsteps moving away, I let out the breath I'd been holding in a long, slow stream.

The competition. They were talking about the annual swimming competition, the one every young creature in our valley had been preparing for. The one I'd been training for every morning in the hidden pools beyond the orchard.

But what weren't we supposed to know?

I slipped down from my perch and walked deeper into the orchard, where the trees grew thicker and the light turned golden-green through the canopy. Each step felt heavy, like walking underwater. The cherry blossoms seemed less cheerful now, more like they were trying to cover something up.

Maybe I'd misunderstood. Maybe they weren't talking about our competition at all. Maybe I'd gotten it completely wrong, the way I sometimes did when I listened to conversations through doors or around corners.

But the feeling in my chest—tight and worried and fluttering like a trapped bird—told me otherwise.

I found myself at the heart of the orchard, where five ancient trees grew in a perfect circle around a clearing carpeted with fallen petals. This was where I always came when I needed to think, though I'd never needed to think about anything quite this troubling before.

The petals crunched softly under my feet as I sat cross-legged in the center of the circle. Above me, the branches formed a pink and white canopy that filtered the light into something gentle and forgiving. But I didn't feel gentle. I felt like I was carrying someone else's secret, and it was heavier than my sealskin when it was soaked with seawater.

What if the competition wasn't fair? What if something was wrong with it?

Or what if I'd heard wrong, and there was nothing to worry about at all?

The problem with secrets is that once you think you have one, it changes the shape of everything else. The cherry blossoms looked different now, like they were hiding things. The golden light felt less warm. Even the soft carpet of petals seemed like it was covering up something important.

I stood up and brushed the petals from my cloak. Tomorrow I would come back here, I decided. Maybe I would notice something I'd missed today. Maybe the orchard would tell me what I needed to know.

Or maybe I would just sit here and worry about things that weren't even real.

But as I walked home between the flowering trees, I couldn't shake the feeling that something in my small, safe world was about to change.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes hiding behind a cherry tree trunk in a dense tropical jungle setting with layers of green foliage, pink cherry blossoms falling around her, ancient ruins visible through the exotic plants, rich saturated colors with golden light filtering through the canopy
illustrations.alt: Selkie hiding behind a cherry tree, overhearing a conversation

chapters.title: The Second Listening
chapters.content:
I told myself I wasn't going back to spy.

But the next afternoon found me in the same spot anyway, pressed against the same ancient trunk with cherry blossoms in my long black hair. The conversations I'd overheard the day before had circled in my mind like restless seals, never quite surfacing long enough for me to catch their meaning.

This time I noticed things I'd missed before. How the bark was worn smooth in places, as if other creatures had pressed themselves here too. How there was a perfect view of the main path from this angle. How the acoustics of the orchard carried voices from surprisingly far away.

Maybe I wasn't the first one to accidentally overhear things here.

"Did you hear what they're saying about the deep pool?" A different voice today, younger. Nervous.

"That's just rumors," came the reply. "You can't believe everything you hear."

"But what if it's true? What if they really did find something down there?"

My sealskin cloak suddenly felt too warm. The deep pool was where the final race would be held, where the strongest swimmers would dive for the golden kelp that grew in the darkest depths. I'd been practicing there every morning, holding my breath longer and longer, my seal nature giving me an advantage most others didn't have.

"Even if they did," the second voice said, "it doesn't change anything for us. We still have to swim our best."

"Unless they cancel the whole thing."

Cancel? My deep sea-dark eyes widened. Why would they cancel the competition?

The voices faded as the speakers moved away, but I stayed pressed against the tree, trying to make sense of what I'd heard. Something in the deep pool. Something that might cancel everything.

I slipped away from my hiding spot and made my way to the center of the orchard again, to the circle of five trees. But this time I approached from a different angle, and I noticed something strange.

One of the trees was younger than the others. Its bark was smoother, its roots not as deep. It looked like it belonged, but when I really looked, I could see it was different.

I sat down in the clearing and tried to think. The petals fell around me like snow, but pink snow that smelled of spring and secrets. What if the conversations I'd been overhearing weren't random? What if this place was where creatures came specifically to share information they didn't want spread around?

What if I was sitting in the middle of some kind of informal message center?

The thought made me feel both clever and guilty at the same time. Clever for figuring it out, guilty for taking advantage of it.

I pulled my sealskin cloak closer and watched the light change from golden to amber as afternoon stretched toward evening. The competition was still weeks away, but everyone was training hard. Every morning, young creatures gathered at the various pools and streams throughout the valley, racing each other and practicing their dives.

But what if we were all preparing for something that wasn't going to happen?

Or worse—what if we were preparing for something that was going to happen, but not the way we expected?

I stood up and walked slowly around the circle of trees, trailing my fingers along their bark. The newest tree felt different under my touch—not wrong, exactly, but not quite right either. Like it was trying very hard to fit in.

Kind of like me, actually.

As I headed home through the orchard, the cherry blossoms seemed to whisper around me. But I couldn't tell if they were trying to warn me about something, or if they were just commenting on the weather.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes sitting in a circle of five cherry trees with pink petals carpeting the ground, dense tropical jungle surroundings with layers of green foliage, ancient stone ruins partially visible through exotic plants, rich saturated colors and golden light filtering through the canopy creating mysterious shadows
illustrations.alt: Selkie contemplating in the circular clearing

chapters.title: What the Petals Remembered
chapters.content:
The third day, I brought my swimming practice to the orchard.

Not actual swimming, of course. But I practiced my breathing exercises in the circle of trees, holding my breath for longer and longer counts while the cherry blossoms drifted down around me. It seemed like a good excuse for being there, in case anyone asked.

Nobody asked.

But as I sat there with my eyes closed, counting the seconds and feeling my selkie nature help me stretch each breath, I heard something new. Not voices this time—footsteps.

Slow, careful footsteps that stopped and started, like someone was looking for something. Or checking to see if they were being watched.

I opened my deep sea-dark eyes just enough to peer through my long black hair. A figure was moving between the trees, stopping at each one and... leaving something?

I held perfectly still until the footsteps moved away, then crept closer to investigate.

Tied to one of the trees was a small piece of bark with scratches carved into it. I couldn't read the scratches, but they looked intentional. Purposeful.

I checked the other trees in the circle. Each one had a piece of bark tied to its trunk, each with different markings.

This wasn't just a place where creatures came to gossip. This was a place where they left messages.

I settled back in the center of the clearing and tried to look like I was just enjoying the falling petals while actually studying the message system I'd stumbled into. The newest tree—the one that looked like it was trying too hard to fit in—had the most recent-looking piece of bark.

Maybe that's why it had been planted. To give the message-leavers more space.

The cherry blossoms kept falling, and I kept pretending to meditate while actually thinking furiously. If this was a message system, then the conversations I'd overheard might not have been accidents. Maybe creatures came here specifically to share information, both by talking and by leaving written messages.

Which meant I'd stumbled into the middle of some kind of informal news network.

The problem was, I couldn't read the messages, and the spoken conversations only gave me fragments. Something about the deep pool. Something about the competition. Something that might or might not be true.

I was collecting pieces of a puzzle, but I didn't have enough pieces to see the whole picture.

As the afternoon light shifted through the pink canopy, I noticed something else. The fallen petals weren't distributed evenly around the clearing. They were thicker in some spots, thinner in others, like they'd been disturbed by frequent foot traffic.

I followed the thickest concentration of petals and found myself at the base of the oldest tree, the one whose roots ran deepest. Someone had been standing here recently, often enough to scatter the petals in a distinctive pattern.

This was the listening spot. The place where creatures came to hear the conversations that happened near the newer trees.

I was standing in someone else's secret place.

The realization made my sealskin cloak feel too tight. What if I was interfering with something important? What if by being here, I was disrupting a system that creatures depended on for information?

But what if the information I was accidentally collecting was important too?

I walked home slowly, taking a different path through the orchard. This route showed me things I hadn't seen before: more carved messages tied to trees, worn spots in the earth where creatures had walked repeatedly, even what looked like a small shelter built into a grove of particularly thick trees.

The orchard wasn't just a place where cherry trees grew. It was a place where information grew too, spreading from tree to tree and creature to creature in ways I was only beginning to understand.

And somehow, I'd become part of that network whether I meant to or not.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes examining carved bark messages tied to ancient cherry trees, dense tropical jungle environment with layers of green foliage, exotic plants growing around stone ruins, golden light filtering through the canopy illuminating the mysterious message system, rich saturated colors creating an atmosphere of discovery
illustrations.alt: Selkie discovering the message system in the trees

chapters.title: The Deep Pool's Secret
chapters.content:
On the fourth day, I didn't go to the orchard at all.

Instead, I went to the deep pool where the final competition would be held. If something was wrong there, something that might cancel the whole event, maybe I could figure out what it was by looking for myself.

The pool lay hidden in a grove beyond the orchard, surrounded by trees so old their roots had grown together into living walls. The water was dark and still, reflecting the canopy above like a mirror made of secrets.

I'd been here dozens of times for practice, but today I tried to see it the way someone else might. Someone who was looking for problems.

The golden kelp still swayed in the depths, as far as I could tell. The water seemed clean and clear. The stone ledges where spectators would sit during the competition looked sturdy.

But as I walked around the pool's edge, I noticed things that seemed wrong. Scratches in the stone that looked too regular to be natural. Patches of algae that grew in strange patterns. Most unsettling of all, places where the water seemed slightly different—clearer in some spots, cloudier in others.

I sat on the diving rock and let my deep sea-dark eyes adjust to the shifting light on the water's surface. My sealskin cloak felt comfortable here, the way it always did near deep water. This was where I belonged, where my selkie nature made me stronger and more confident than anywhere else.

So why did something feel wrong?

I slipped into the water without making a splash, letting my body remember how to move like a seal. The pool welcomed me the way it always did, cool and embracing and full of gentle currents.

But as I dove deeper, following the familiar path toward the golden kelp, I saw something that made me forget to keep swimming.

The kelp wasn't growing naturally. It had been planted.

I could see where someone had carefully arranged the roots among the rocks, positioning each strand for maximum effect. The golden color that made it so prized? It came from metal threads woven through the natural kelp.

The treasure we were supposed to compete for was fake.

I surfaced gasping, not from lack of air but from the shock of understanding. No wonder there were rumors about canceling the competition. No wonder creatures were leaving worried messages in the orchard.

Someone had discovered that the prize wasn't real.

I floated on my back and tried to think. Who would do this? And why? The competition had been held for generations, with young creatures diving for genuine golden kelp that grew naturally in the deepest pools.

Unless... maybe it had never been natural. Maybe the kelp had always been planted, and this year someone had finally figured it out.

Or maybe this was the first year someone had tried to fake it, and they'd done a bad job.

I dove down again, this time looking more carefully at everything around the kelp. The scratches in the stone weren't random—they were tool marks. Someone had been doing construction work down here, installing or removing or rearranging things.

When I surfaced the second time, I wasn't alone.

"You're not supposed to be here," a voice said from the pool's edge.

I turned toward the sound and saw an older creature I recognized but couldn't quite name. Someone official-looking. Someone who probably had good reasons for not wanting young selkies poking around in competition pools.

"I was just practicing," I said, which was sort of true.

"The pool is closed for maintenance," they replied. "Didn't you see the notice?"

I looked around. There was no notice anywhere that I could see.

"Oh," I said. "I guess I missed it."

They watched me climb out of the water, their expression unreadable. "The competition is still weeks away," they said. "Plenty of time to practice in the other pools."

I nodded and wrapped my soft grey-brown sealskin cloak around my shoulders. "Is everything all right with this pool?"

"Everything's fine," they said quickly. "Just routine maintenance. Nothing to worry about."

But the way they said it made me worry more, not less.

As I walked back toward the orchard, I realized I now had more pieces of the puzzle. But they still didn't fit together in a way that made sense.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes floating in dark water surrounded by artificial golden kelp with metal threads, underwater scene in dense tropical jungle setting with ancient stone ruins visible through the water, layers of green foliage above the surface, golden light filtering down through the canopy creating mysterious underwater illumination, rich saturated colors
illustrations.alt: Selkie discovering the artificial kelp underwater

chapters.title: Messages in the Bark
chapters.content:
I returned to the orchard that evening, but this time I came with a purpose.

The carved messages on the trees had seemed like meaningless scratches before, but now I wondered if I'd been looking at them wrong. Maybe they weren't words in any language I knew. Maybe they were pictures, or symbols, or some other kind of code.

I settled in the center of the five-tree circle and studied each message systematically. The light was different in the evening—softer, more golden, casting shadows that made the scratches deeper and clearer.

On the newest tree, the marks looked like wavy lines. Water, maybe?

On the oldest tree, something that might have been a circle with lines radiating from it. The sun? Or something else?

I was so focused on trying to decode the messages that I almost missed the sound of approaching voices. Almost.

"—checked the pool again today," someone was saying. "Found another one poking around."

"What did you tell them?"

"Same thing we've been telling everyone. Routine maintenance. Nothing to worry about."

I pressed myself against the newest tree, the one whose message I'd been trying to read. The bark felt rough against my sealskin cloak.

"How much longer can we keep this up?"

"Until we figure out what to do. The competition is supposed to happen in two weeks. We can't just cancel it without explaining why."

"But we can't hold it with fake kelp either. Everyone will know."

"Maybe not everyone. Maybe just the ones who win."

There was a long pause. When the conversation resumed, the voices were quieter, harder to hear.

"What if we planted real kelp to replace the fake stuff?"

"Where would we get it? Real golden kelp doesn't just grow anywhere."

"I know someone who might know where to find some..."

The voices moved away, but I stayed pressed against the tree until I was sure they were gone. My heart was beating so fast it felt like it might jump out of my chest and swim away without me.

Now I understood. The fake kelp hadn't been planted to cheat anyone. It had been planted to replace real kelp that had died or been removed. Someone was trying to save the competition, not sabotage it.

But they were doing it in secret, which meant they thought they would get in trouble if anyone found out.

I looked at the message carved into the tree I was leaning against. The wavy lines that I'd thought might represent water... what if they represented kelp? And the radiating circle on the oldest tree... what if that represented the pool?

What if the messages weren't random information sharing, but specific updates about the competition situation?

I moved from tree to tree, studying each carved message with my new theory in mind. Wavy lines, circles, crossed lines, dots arranged in patterns... it could be a simple code for tracking the status of different aspects of the competition.

Which meant other creatures knew about the fake kelp. Maybe lots of other creatures.

Maybe I wasn't the only one who had stumbled into this secret.

As the evening light faded through the pink blossoms above, I tried to imagine how many young creatures like me were training for a competition that might not happen, or might not be what they expected. How many were worried, how many had heard rumors, how many were trying to figure out the truth from fragments and whispers.

The cherry blossoms fell around me like pink snow, each petal carrying its own small secret. But now I wondered if the secrets were lighter than I'd thought. Maybe they weren't meant to hurt anyone. Maybe they were just complicated, and the creatures trying to solve the problems didn't want to worry everyone else while they worked.

I stood up and brushed the petals from my long black hair. Tomorrow, I decided, I would try a different approach. Instead of just collecting pieces of information, I would try to do something helpful with what I'd learned.

Though I wasn't entirely sure what that might be yet.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes studying carved symbols on tree bark in evening light, dense tropical jungle setting with layers of green foliage, cherry blossoms falling around her, ancient ruins partially visible through exotic plants, golden light filtering through the canopy creating dramatic shadows that highlight the mysterious carved messages, rich saturated colors
illustrations.alt: Selkie decoding the carved messages in evening light

chapters.title: The Weight of Knowing
chapters.content:
I didn't sleep well that night.

Every time I closed my deep sea-dark eyes, I saw fake kelp swaying in dark water, metal threads glinting like lies in the depths. I saw worried creatures carving messages into bark, trying to solve a problem they couldn't talk about openly.

Mostly, I saw the other young creatures in our valley, training hard every day for a competition that might not be what they thought it was.

By morning, I'd made up my mind. I was going to find someone in charge and tell them what I knew. Not to get anyone in trouble, but to help fix whatever was wrong.

The problem was figuring out who was actually in charge.

I made my way back to the orchard, but this time I took the long path that circled around the entire grove. I wanted to see if there were other message trees, other gathering spots, other pieces of the network I'd discovered.

What I found was more complicated than I'd expected.

The message system extended far beyond the five-tree circle. Almost every large tree in the orchard had at least one carved piece of bark tied to its trunk. Some had multiple messages, layered like the pages of a book.

And there were creatures tending to them.

I watched from behind a flowering bush as an elderly creature I didn't recognize moved methodically from tree to tree, checking messages, replacing some with new ones, making notes on a piece of bark they carried.

This wasn't just informal information sharing. This was organized. Official, even.

Maybe the creatures trying to solve the kelp problem weren't working in secret because they were doing something wrong. Maybe they were working in secret because it was their job to solve problems without worrying everyone else.

The thought made me feel both better and worse at the same time.

Better because it meant the adults in our valley were working to fix things, and they probably knew what they were doing.

Worse because it meant I'd been spying on creatures who were just trying to do their jobs.

I was so lost in thought that I didn't notice another creature approaching until they spoke.

"You've been spending a lot of time in the orchard lately."

I turned and saw the same official-looking creature who'd found me at the deep pool. They didn't look angry, exactly, but they looked like they wanted answers.

"I like the cherry blossoms," I said, which was true.

"So do a lot of creatures," they replied. "But most of them don't sit in the same spot every day, or study the message trees, or practice their breathing exercises in the exact center of the information exchange circle."

Information exchange circle. So it did have an official name.

"I didn't mean to cause any problems," I said.

"You haven't caused problems," they said. "But you've definitely noticed some. The question is: what do you plan to do with what you've noticed?"

I looked at my feet, where fallen cherry blossoms were already gathering on my soft grey-brown sealskin cloak. "I just want the competition to be fair," I said finally. "And I want to understand what's really happening."

The creature studied me for a long moment. Then they sat down on a fallen log and gestured for me to join them.

"What do you think is really happening?" they asked.

"I think the kelp in the deep pool isn't natural," I said carefully. "And I think some creatures are trying to fix that problem before the competition. And I think they're trying to do it without making everyone worry."

The creature nodded slowly. "That's... not entirely wrong," they said. "Though it's more complicated than that."

"How is it more complicated?"

They picked up a fallen blossom and turned it over in their hands. "What if I told you that the kelp has never been entirely natural? That every year, we've had to supplement what grows naturally with cultivated kelp, because there isn't enough for a proper competition?"

I stared at them. "But that would mean..."

"That would mean the competition has always been partly artificial, and this year we're just trying to maintain the tradition."

The cherry blossoms suddenly looked different again, like they were settling over a world that wasn't quite what I'd thought it was.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes sitting on a fallen log with an official creature among cherry blossom trees, dense tropical jungle environment with layers of green foliage, ancient ruins visible in background, extensive message system of carved bark visible on multiple trees, golden light filtering through the canopy, rich saturated colors creating a scene of revelation and understanding
illustrations.alt: Selkie having a serious conversation in the orchard

chapters.title: Truth in the Currents
chapters.content:
"But everyone thinks it's natural," I said.

The creature nodded, still turning the cherry blossom in their hands. "Everyone thinks a lot of things that aren't quite true. That's not always bad. Sometimes believing in something makes it more real than it would be otherwise."

I tried to wrap my mind around this idea. "So the competition is real, even if the kelp isn't entirely natural?"

"The swimming is real. The training is real. The achievement of reaching the kelp is real. Does it matter if we help the kelp grow where young creatures can actually reach it?"

"I don't know," I admitted. "It feels like it should matter. But I'm not sure why."

The creature smiled, and their expression was gentler than I'd expected. "That's a good answer. It shows you're thinking about it properly."

We sat in comfortable silence for a while, watching the pink petals drift down around us. The orchard felt different now that I knew about the message system, now that I understood it was part of something larger and more organized than I'd realized.

"Can I ask you something?" I said finally.

"Of course."

"Why do you let creatures leave messages and have conversations here if the information might not be accurate? Some of what I overheard sounded like rumors and speculation."

The creature considered this. "What do you think happens to rumors when they're shared openly versus when they're kept secret?"

I thought about all the fragments of conversation I'd collected, all the worry and uncertainty I'd felt. "When they're secret, they feel more important. More scary."

"And when they're shared openly?"

"Other creatures can correct them if they're wrong. Or add information that makes them make more sense."

"Exactly. The orchard isn't just for sharing information. It's for testing information. Seeing how it sounds when it's spoken out loud, how it feels when it's carved into bark and left for others to find."

I looked around at all the message trees with new understanding. "So you let creatures share rumors and speculation because that's how you figure out what's true?"

"Among other things, yes. And because keeping secrets is exhausting. Sometimes creatures need a place to voice their worries, even if those worries turn out to be unnecessary."

I pulled my sealskin cloak closer and thought about all the worrying I'd done over the past few days. It had been exhausting.

"What happens now?" I asked. "With the competition, I mean."

"Now we do what we always do. We make sure there's enough kelp for everyone who dives deep enough to reach it. We make sure the pool is safe. We make sure the competition is as fair and meaningful as we can make it."

"Even if the kelp isn't entirely natural?"

"Especially then. If we're going to supplement nature, we have a responsibility to do it well."

The creature stood up and brushed the petals from their clothes. "You're welcome to keep coming to the orchard, you know. Now that you understand what it's for, you might find it more useful and less mysterious."

"What if I want to leave a message?"

"Then you learn to carve, and you find yourself a piece of bark, and you tie it to whichever tree feels right for your message."

They started to walk away, then paused. "Oh, and one more thing. The young creature who's been training in the early mornings at the deep pool? The one who can hold her breath longer than anyone expected?"

"Yes?"

"She's going to do very well in the competition, regardless of where the kelp came from."

I watched them disappear between the trees, their words settling around me like the ever-falling cherry blossoms.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes sitting thoughtfully among falling cherry blossoms in a dense tropical jungle setting, layers of green foliage surrounding her, ancient stone ruins partially visible, golden light filtering through the canopy creating a peaceful contemplative atmosphere, rich saturated colors with pink petals drifting through the air
illustrations.alt: Selkie contemplating the nature of truth among falling blossoms

chapters.title: Learning to Carve
chapters.content:
The next morning, I didn't go to the deep pool to practice swimming.

Instead, I spent the early hours learning to carve.

I'd found a piece of bark that had fallen naturally from one of the cherry trees, and I sat in the center of the five-tree circle with a sharp stone, trying to figure out how to turn my thoughts into marks that other creatures could understand.

It was harder than I'd expected.

The first few attempts looked like random scratches. The stone kept slipping in my hands, and the bark kept splitting in the wrong places. My deep sea-dark eyes strained to see the fine details in the shifting light.

But gradually, I began to understand the rhythm of it. Press gently, move slowly, let the stone find its own path through the bark's grain.

What I wanted to carve was something about questions. About how sometimes the most important thing wasn't getting the right answers, but learning to ask better questions.

What I actually managed to carve looked like a wavy line with dots around it.

Close enough.

I was trying to decide which tree to tie my message to when I heard familiar voices approaching. This time, though, I didn't hide. Instead, I stayed where I was and waited to see what would happen.

"Oh," one of the voices said as they entered the clearing. "Hello."

I looked up to see two young creatures about my own age. I recognized them from the practice pools, though I'd never spoken to either of them.

"I hope I'm not in your way," I said.

"Not at all," the second creature replied. "We just come here sometimes to... well, to talk about things."

"Competition things," the first one added. "We heard there might be problems."

I held up my carved piece of bark. "I heard that too. I was just trying to figure out how to ask better questions about it."

They looked at each other, then at me, then at the message trees around us.

"You know about the messages?" the second creature asked.

"I just found out what they were for," I said. "Before that, I thought I was accidentally overhearing secrets."

"Weren't you?"

"Sort of. But it turns out the secrets weren't what I thought they were."

I told them about the fake kelp, and the official creatures trying to solve the problem, and the conversation I'd had with the creature who'd found me. I told them about the tradition of supplementing natural kelp, and the question of whether that made the competition less meaningful or more.

They listened without interrupting, which I appreciated.

When I finished, the first creature said, "So we're still going to have the competition?"

"Yes. And it's still going to be fair. It's just... more complicated than we thought."

"Everything's more complicated than we think," the second creature said. "That's what my grandmother always says."

We sat together in the clearing, each of us lost in our own thoughts. The cherry blossoms kept falling, landing in our hair and on our clothes and on the ground around us like pink reminders that beautiful things don't last forever, but they keep coming back.

"I think I want to leave a message too," the first creature said eventually.

"Me too," said the second.

So we spent the rest of the morning learning to carve together, helping each other when the stone slipped or the bark split wrong. We talked about the competition, and about training, and about how strange it felt to learn that adults were working behind the scenes to make things possible.

"It makes me feel safer," the first creature said. "Knowing that someone's paying attention to the details."

"It makes me feel more responsible," the second one added. "Like I should pay attention to the details too."

I looked at my own carved message, still imperfect but clearer now than when I'd started. "It makes me feel like I'm part of something bigger," I said.

When we were done carving, we tied our messages to three different trees. Mine went on the newest tree, because it felt like the right place for questions about new ways of understanding old traditions.

Theirs went on trees they chose for their own reasons, which they didn't have to explain to me.

As we walked home together, the orchard felt different again. Not mysterious and threatening, not even just beautiful. It felt like a place where creatures came to figure things out together, one conversation and one carved message at a time.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes sitting with two other young creatures learning to carve messages on bark pieces, dense tropical jungle setting with layers of green foliage, cherry blossoms falling around them, ancient ruins visible through exotic plants, golden light filtering through the canopy illuminating their collaborative work, rich saturated colors creating a scene of friendship and discovery
illustrations.alt: Selkie learning to carve with new friends in the orchard

chapters.title: Diving Deep
chapters.content:
The competition was held on a bright morning two weeks later.

I stood at the edge of the deep pool with the other young creatures, my soft grey-brown sealskin cloak folded neatly on the stone ledge behind me. The water was dark and still, reflecting the canopy above like a mirror that held secrets and dreams in equal measure.

The kelp swayed far below, golden threads catching what little light penetrated to those depths. I couldn't tell which strands were natural and which were cultivated, and I realized that was exactly the point.

It didn't matter where the kelp came from. What mattered was whether I could dive deep enough to reach it.

The official creatures had explained the rules the same way they always did. Dive deep, retrieve a strand of kelp, surface safely. The quality of your dive and the depth you achieved would be judged along with your success in the task itself.

Nothing about the explanation suggested that this year was different from any other year.

Except that I knew it was, and somehow that made it feel more important, not less.

When my turn came, I dove cleanly from the stone platform, letting my body remember how to be seal-like in the water. The pool welcomed me with its familiar coolness, its gentle currents guiding me downward toward the golden kelp.

As I descended, I thought about all the creatures who had made this moment possible. The ones who grew and tended the kelp, both natural and cultivated. The ones who maintained the pool and ensured it was safe for diving. The ones who carved messages and shared information and worried about details so that young creatures like me could focus on swimming our best.

The kelp was exactly where it was supposed to be, swaying gently in the deep currents. I chose a strand that felt right under my hands—not too thick, not too thin, growing from roots that could spare it.

It didn't occur to me until I was swimming back toward the surface that I'd chosen one of the natural strands. I could feel the difference somehow, the way it moved in the water, the way it had grown according to its own patterns rather than being placed according to someone else's plan.

But when I surfaced, kelp in hand, gasping and exhilarated and proud, I realized that the choice hadn't mattered as much as I'd thought it would.

What mattered was that I'd made the dive successfully. What mattered was the training I'd done, the courage I'd found, the moment of floating in the deep water with kelp in my hands and sunlight filtering down from far above.

What mattered was that I'd learned to ask better questions about the things I thought I understood.

As I climbed out of the pool and wrapped my sealskin cloak around my shoulders, I caught sight of several creatures I recognized from the orchard. The official creature who'd explained the message system to me. The elderly creature who tended the carved bark communications. Even the two young creatures I'd learned to carve with, who had completed their own dives earlier in the day.

They were all smiling, and their smiles looked like they meant the same thing mine felt like.

We had participated in something real and meaningful and beautiful, even though it was more complicated than we'd originally understood.

Maybe especially because it was more complicated.

Later, as the day wound down and creatures began to head home through the orchard, I made one last stop at the five-tree circle. The cherry blossoms were nearly finished for the year, just a few stragglers clinging to the branches and a thick carpet of pink petals covering the ground.

But the message trees were full of new communications, carved pieces of bark that recorded the day's events, shared observations and congratulations, posed new questions for future consideration.

I found the piece of bark I'd carved weeks ago, still tied to the newest tree. The wavy line with dots around it looked different to me now, though the carving itself hadn't changed.

Maybe that's how understanding works. The information stays the same, but we learn to see it from new angles, in different light, with better questions shaping how we interpret what we observe.

I added my name to the bark, carefully scratched below my original design. Not because the message needed my name to be complete, but because I wanted to claim my part in the ongoing conversation.

Then I walked home through the orchard, my sealskin cloak still damp with pool water, my long black hair still decorated with a few cherry blossoms that had fallen during the day's festivities.

The competition was over, but the questions I'd learned to ask were just beginning to show me new ways of understanding the world I lived in.

And somehow, that felt like the most valuable prize of all.

illustrations.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes diving underwater toward golden kelp in dark depths, dense tropical jungle setting visible above the water surface with layers of green foliage and ancient ruins, golden light filtering down through the water creating dramatic underwater illumination, rich saturated colors showing both natural and artificial kelp swaying in the currents
illustrations.alt: Selkie diving toward the golden kelp in the competition

metadata.educationalValue: Explores how information spreads, the difference between rumors and facts, and the complexity of truth in community settings.
metadata.parentGuide: A gentle story about discovering that adult systems are more complex than they appear, with themes of media literacy woven through a magical sports narrative.

coverPrompt.description: A young selkie with long black hair, soft grey-brown sealskin cloak, and deep sea-dark eyes stands among pink cherry blossoms in a dense tropical jungle setting with layers of green foliage, hidden paths visible in the background, ancient stone ruins partially covered by exotic plants, rich saturated colors with golden light filtering through the canopy creating an atmosphere of adventure and exploration
coverPrompt.alt: Selkie in a mystical cherry orchard

questions.question: Why does Selkie first start visiting the cherry orchard regularly?
choices: She accidentally overhears a conversation and becomes curious
questions.explanation: Selkie first overhears a conversation by accident while enjoying the cherry blossoms, and this makes her curious enough to return.

questions.question: What does Selkie discover about the golden kelp in the competition pool?
choices: It's been supplemented with cultivated kelp for years
questions.explanation: Selkie learns that the competition has always used a combination of natural and cultivated kelp to ensure there's enough for all the competitors.

questions.question: What is the five-tree circle really used for?
questions.explanation: The orchard serves as an organized information exchange where creatures share news, concerns, and messages through both conversation and carved bark messages.

questions.question: How does Selkie's understanding of the overheard conversations change throughout the story?
choices: She realizes they were about a different competition
She learns the adults are trying to solve problems, not create them
She discovers they were completely made up
She finds out they were meant to mislead her
questions.explanation: Selkie initially thinks she's discovered something wrong, but learns the adults are working to maintain the competition tradition responsibly.

questions.question: What does the official creature tell Selkie about sharing rumors openly versus keeping them secret?
choices: Rumors should always be kept secret
When rumors are shared openly, others can correct or add to them
Rumors always turn out to be true
questions.explanation: The official explains that sharing rumors and speculation openly allows communities to test information and correct misinformation.

questions.question: Why does Selkie decide to learn carving?
choices: To leave her own message in the information system
questions.explanation: After understanding how the message system works, Selkie wants to participate by sharing her own thoughts and questions.

questions.question: What is the main theme about information that runs through the story?
choices: Understanding truth requires asking better questions and considering multiple perspectives
questions.explanation: The story demonstrates that truth is often complex and requires critical thinking, good questions, and open communication to understand properly.

questions.question: How does Selkie feel about the competition after learning the kelp isn't entirely natural?
choices: She realizes it's still meaningful because of the skill and effort required
She wants to expose the secret
questions.explanation: Selkie comes to understand that the competition's value lies in the training, courage, and achievement of the participants, regardless of how the kelp is sourced.
